does: This paper presents a method for improving summarization quality by training reward models on human preference comparisons and using reinforcement learning to optimize summarization policies against these learned rewards. The authors collect a large dataset of human judgments comparing summary pairs, train a neural reward model to predict which summary humans prefer, and then use this reward model to fine-tune a summarization policy via RL. Testing on Reddit's TL;DR dataset, their approach substantially outperforms both supervised baselines trained on reference summaries and larger models trained with standard supervised learning. The method also demonstrates strong transfer to CNN/DailyMail news articles without task-specific fine-tuning, indicating the learned preferences generalize across domains.
connects: This work extends recent efforts to improve language model training by optimizing for human feedback rather than reference data or automatic metrics like ROUGE. It builds on reinforcement learning from human feedback techniques previously applied to other language generation tasks and combines this with the observation that ROUGE and reference summaries are imperfect proxies for actual summary quality. The paper responds to the fundamental bottleneck in NLP training—the scarcity and cost of high-quality labeled data—by inverting the problem to collect comparative preference judgments, which are cheaper to obtain and more directly encode human quality judgments than absolute reference summaries. This approach also relates to broader work on moving beyond maximum likelihood training for generation tasks.
enables: This work establishes human feedback as a scalable training signal for text generation and opens pathways for improving large language models across many tasks where automatic metrics fail to capture true quality. The success of preference-based learning suggests future work could apply similar methods to translation, question-answering, dialogue, and other open-ended generation problems. The transferability of learned preferences across datasets suggests the approach might enable building more general-purpose reward models applicable across multiple tasks or domains. Additionally, the method provides a framework for aligning increasingly powerful models with human values, pointing toward techniques for AI safety and controllability that could become critical as models grow larger and their impacts more significant.
